dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911?
I've been having trouble breathing for about four hours.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and the date?
Yes, I'm Sarah, and the date is September 15th.
Can you tell me where you are right now?
I'm at home in [CITY].
What is your age, please?
I'm 63 years of age.
If you can hear me, squeeze my hand.
(squeezes the hand) There.
We need to check your airway now. Can you speak easily?
Respirations are fast but not shallow.
SpO₂ is 100% while on 3 liters of oxygen at home.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation Pulse rate and quality
dialogue.utterance: Pulse is very fast-124 beats per minute.
Along with the breathing, have you felt any other symptoms today?
I'm just struggling to take deep breaths.
Do you have any known allergies to medications or foods?
No known allergies.
What medications are you currently taking?
I have Spiriva, Symbicort, Atrovent, Albuterol, Prednisone, and Tramadol.
Can you tell us about your past medical history?
I have COPD, asthma, and emphysema.
When did you last eat or drink anything?
I think it was about six hours ago.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Is there anything that started this or made it worse recently?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: My home oxygen isn't helping much now. I also tried a nebulizer, but I'm still struggling.
Pulse is 124 beats per minute.
Respirations are at 28 per minute.
Blood pressure is 126 over 71.
SpO₂ remains at 100% on 3 liters.
We couldn't get a glucose reading-we're out of testing strips at the moment.
EKG was not performed due to equipment unavailability.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam Pupillary reflex
dialogue.utterance: I'm going to shine a light into your eyes-this is just to check the pupils.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam Pupillary reflex
dialogue.utterance: Go ahead.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam Pupillary reflex
dialogue.utterance: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam Neck veins
dialogue.utterance: No signs of jugular venous distention noted.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam Mental status
dialogue.utterance: What date is it today?

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam Mental status
dialogue.utterance: September 15th.
Skin is warm and dry.
I'm going to listen to your lungs to get a better idea.
Breath sounds are significantly diminished, with very little air movement in both lungs.
Do you have any pain anywhere in your abdomen or between your chest and waistline?
I'm not having any abdominal pain.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam Peripheral pulses
dialogue.utterance: Pulses are present in all four of your extremities.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam Peripheral motor/sensory status
dialogue.utterance: The patient's peripheral motor and sensory status appears intact and normal.
We're going to give you a nebulizer treatment right now.
We'll be giving you Albuterol and Atrovent now via a nebulizer to help open your airways.
You'll feel a little mist-just breathe normally and take deep breaths.
That's helping a bit-thank you.
I'm going to try starting an IV in your arm for possible additional support.
Unfortunately, the IV attempt didn't work-we'll continue focusing on breathing treatments.
Based on your condition and response to treatment, I'm following the respiratory distress protocol.
Your pulse is now down to 108 per minute.
Respirations remain at 28 per minute-still elevated but steady.
Blood pressure is 116 over 70 now.
SpO₂ stays at 100% on high concentration oxygen support.
We didn't have a complete reading, but it's noted in the report as zero.
Another round of nebulizer therapy was performed with some improvement noted in breathing status.
The patient has no reported pain at this time.
She's not exhibiting any signs of pain, which is good to note.
We're going to take you to [COMPANY_NAME] System. Is that alright?
That works for me.
I'm placing dry pads beneath you now-I'll secure you to the stretcher safely.
You're all ready and secured-everything's set for transport.
We're all ready-let's go once everything's in place.
We have a 63-year-old female who is AAOx4 with intact ABCs and a Glasgow Coma Scale of 15.
Medical history includes COPD, asthma, and emphysema. She's currently in tripoding and in respiratory distress.
Vital signs improved-pulse is down to 108 and SpO₂ remains at 100% on high concentration oxygen.
The patient shows no signs of pain and has good peripheral motor movement with no edema or trauma to extremities.
She is not in JVD, and her pupils are equal, round, and reactive. She reported some improvement in ease of breathing.
She has explicitly stated she does not want CPAP at this time.
Thanks for the thorough report-she's coming in now for further care.
The patient is alert, oriented, and in respiratory distress but ABCs remain intact.
The patient has intact peripheral motor function with no signs of pain, trauma, or edema.
Skin has a normal appearance, warm and dry.
You're secure for the transport to [COMPANY_NAME]-let's move her now.
